coredata.dc:title: Co3O4/CQDs composite for selective ammonia detection in exhaled human breath analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Jiapeng
Fu, Lijuan
Liu, Chaoqiang
Liu, Qingyu
Tang, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Co3O4 was synthesized using electrospinning method and different amounts of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were subsequently doped into the Co3O4 matrix via grinding, resulting in Co3O4/CQDs composite materials with various ratios. Gas sensing tests revealed that the resulting composite Co3O4/CQDs sensors exhibited excellent performance at a low temperature of 50°C, with outstanding selectivity towards ammonia gas and rapid response and recovery characteristics. Specifically, response and recovery time for 50 ppm ammonia gas were 14 s and 164 s, respectively. The outcomes of the exhalation simulation test utilizing sensors composed of this material demonstrate that the sensor's response to simulated chronic kidney disease breath closely approximates that of pure biological samples (1.08), exceeding the average response value of healthy exhaled breath samples (1.0). This demonstrates the sensor's capability to differentiate between healthy individuals and chronic kidney disease patients despite significant water vapor and carbon dioxide interference. This study provides valuable insights for the development of more efficient ammonia sensors and their role in the early detection of chronic kidney disease.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Co3O4
Co3O4/CQDs
Ammonia sensor
Exhalation test

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The preparation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
Measurement of the simulated breath samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (61801276), and Shandong Province Natural Science Foundation (ZR2018BF003)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detailed preparation processes of pure Co3O4 NFs, CQDs, Co3O4/CQDs composite material.
Schematic diagram of the experimental procedure for simulating breath in chronic kidney disease.

(a) SEM images of Co3O4. (b-d) SEM images of Co3O4/CQD100, Co3O4/CQD300, Co3O4/CQD500. (e) EDS image of Co3O4/CQD100. (f) C element mapping. (g) Co element mapping. (h) O element mapping. (i) EDS elemental spectrum of Co3O4/CQD100. (j) TEM image of Co3O4/CQD100. (k)HRTEM image of Co3O4/CQD100.
XRD spectrum of Co3O4 and Co3O4/CQD.
(a) XPS spectrum of Co3O4/CQD100. (b) XPS fine spectrum of C 1 s. (c) XPS fine spectrum of O 1 s. (d) XPS fine spectrum of Co 2p.

(a) Relationship between the response to 50 ppm ammonia and different operating temperatures for Co3O4, Co3O4/CQD100, Co3O4/CQD300, and Co3O4/CQD500. (b) Dynamic response curves to ammonia in the concentration range of 5–100 ppm for Co3O4, Co3O4/CQD100, Co3O4/CQD300, and Co3O4/CQD500 at the optimal operating temperature. (c) Fitting curves of the response values for Co3O4, Co3O4/CQD100, Co3O4/CQD300, and Co3O4/CQD500. (d) Selectivity of Co3O4, Co3O4/CQD100, Co3O4/CQD300, and Co3O4/CQD500 towards 50 ppm ammonia and other gases at the optimal operating temperature. (e) Reproducibility of Co3O4/CQD100 towards 50 ppm ammonia at 50°C. (f) Response/recovery time of Co3O4/CQD100 towards 50 ppm ammonia at 50°C.

(a) Response of Co3O4/CQD100 sensor exposed to simulated breath from chronic kidney disease patients, 1.5 ppm ammonia, and healthy individual breath at 50°C. (b) Resistance change curves of Co3O4/CQD100 sensor at different relative humidity levels. (c) Long-term stability of Co3O4/CQD100 sensor towards 50 ppm ammonia at 50°C.
Comparison of the ammonia sensing properties of Co3O4/CQDs with various Co3O4-based sensors reported in the literature.

head.ce:title: Co3O4/CQDs composite for selective ammonia detection in exhaled human breath analysis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Electronics, Shandong Normal University, Jinan, Shandong Province 250014, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Electronics, Shandong Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Nanofibers and Nanotechnology, National University of Singapore, Singapore 119077, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center for Nanofibers and Nanotechnology, National University of Singapore

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center for Nanofibers and Nanotechnology, National University of Singapore, Singapore, 119077, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Electronics, Shandong Normal University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed to the work equally and should be regarded as co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Co3O4 was synthesized using electrospinning method and different amounts of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were subsequently doped into the Co3O4 matrix via grinding, resulting in Co3O4/CQDs composite materials with various ratios. Gas sensing tests revealed that the resulting composite Co3O4/CQDs sensors exhibited excellent performance at a low temperature of 50°C, with outstanding selectivity towards ammonia gas and rapid response and recovery characteristics. Specifically, response and recovery time for 50 ppm ammonia gas were 14 s and 164 s, respectively. The outcomes of the exhalation simulation test utilizing sensors composed of this material demonstrate that the sensor's response to simulated chronic kidney disease breath closely approximates that of pure biological samples (1.08), exceeding the average response value of healthy exhaled breath samples (1.0). This demonstrates the sensor's capability to differentiate between healthy individuals and chronic kidney disease patients despite significant water vapor and carbon dioxide interference. This study provides valuable insights for the development of more efficient ammonia sensors and their role in the early detection of chronic kidney disease.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Exhaled breath examination has emerged as a central area of exploration in medical diagnostics, bearing significant importance for early non-invasive disease detection[1–3]. Human exhaled breath contains thousands of gases, typically found in concentrations ranging from parts per billion to parts per million. Fluctuations in the levels of specific gas constituents are linked to particular ailments[4,5]. Gas chromatography and mass spectrometry stand as the predominant methods for exhaled breath analysis, offering precise gas component analysis[6–8]. However, these methods are associated with high costs, complexity, and the necessity for pre-concentrating exhaled gases. Consequently, while exhaled breath analysis holds promise in medical diagnostics, its advantages have yet to be fully realized[9,10]. In recent years, considerable efforts have been directed towards gas sensors for exhaled breath analysis[11,12]. Among various types of gas sensors, semiconductor gas sensors are considered the most promising due to their high sensitivity, rapid response, affordability, and integration capabilities[13–17]. Jang et al. combined Oswald-driven single-nozzle electrospinning with sacrificial template routes to successfully fabricate thin-walled SnO2 nanotubes (NTs) suitable for dual-mode pore generation and catalyst loading. Utilizing biomimetic nanocatalysts-functionalized highly porous SnO2 nanotubes, they demonstrated remarkably high gas response to acetone, outstanding selectivity towards other interfering gases, and notably low detection limits (down to 10 ppb) for simulated diabetic acetone molecules. The developed porous SnO2 NT-based sensor array could directly discriminate between simulated diabetic respiration and healthy individuals' normal respiration[16]. He et al. synthesized LaF3–Co3O4 nanomaterials via a facile hydrothermal method, enhancing the low-concentration gas sensing performance of methanol, one of the characteristic gases for detecting lung cancer (LC) lesions. The study revealed that 5 at% LaF3–Co3O4 nanorods exhibited excellent methanol detection capabilities, with a response exceeding 4.0 at 275°C and 75 % relative humidity (RH), even at a concentration of 10−4 methanol. Additionally, the material demonstrated long-term stability (maximum deviation within 15 % over 2 weeks) and outstanding selectivity[17]. Currently, research on exhalation biomarker gas sensors primarily focuses on detecting biological markers for diabetes and respiratory markers for cancer[18,19], with relatively limited investigation into respiratory markers, such as ammonia gas, for chronic kidney disease.

Ammonia (NH3) is present in exhaled breath, originating from metabolic activities in the human body. Ammonia is regarded as a biomarker reflecting the progression of chronic kidney disease in different exhaled breaths. Clinical analyses indicate ammonia concentrations of approximately 0.8 ppm in exhaled breath from healthy individuals, while ammonia concentrations exceed 1.5 ppm in chronic kidney disease patients[20,21]. Nevertheless, distinguishing target gases from complex gas compositions and mitigating the effects of humidity, given the complexity of exhaled breath compositions and relative humidity reaching 100 %, present two major challenges for gas sensors[22–24].

Co3O4 is a typical p-type metal oxide with a spinel structure, encompassing two oxidation states of Co2+ and Co3+. Co2+ ions exhibit high catalytic activity, facilitating redox reactions[25,26]. Due to the variable oxidation states of Co, Co3O4 can adsorb a large amount of oxygen at optimal operating temperatures[25]. This advantage enables its application in various gas sensors[27,28]. However, single-metal oxide sensors often suffer from drawbacks such as high operating temperatures, low response values, and low detection limits. In order to enhance the sensitivity performance of materials, attention has been directed towards carbon quantum dots (CQDs) materials. CQDs are nanomaterials composed of a carbon framework, possessing a high surface area and rapid electron transfer capability[29–31]. Moreover, CQDs feature diverse surface functional groups, enabling selective reactions with specific gases[32]. Consequently, sensors containing CQDs typically exhibit advantages such as low operating temperatures, high sensitivity, and low detection limits[29,33]. Yu et al. prepared CQD/ZnO nanoflower sensors via a hydrothermal method, reducing the operating temperature to below 100°C and lowering the detection limit to less than 100 ppb[33]. Meanwhile, Zhang et al. achieved an ultra-sensitive room-temperature ammonia sensor by assembling CQDs on independent ultrathin coordination polymer (CP) nanosheets (Co[Ni(CN)4]). This sensor demonstrated excellent sensitivity, rapid gas response, significant reproducibility, high selectivity, good long-term stability, and a detection limit as low as 8 ppb for ammonia gas[29].

In this work, the issue of high operating temperature associated with single Co3O4 has been addressed through the development of Co3O4/CQDs composite sensors. This composite material not only effectively lowers the operating temperature to 50°C but also exhibits outstanding ammonia selectivity at this temperature. When exposed to 50 ppm ammonia gas, it achieves a maximum response of 1.5, with no response to other gases. Additionally, humidity testing reveals that the prepared sensor maintains responsiveness to ammonia gas even in high-humidity environments with a relative humidity of up to 85 %. These features suggest the potential of this sensor for detecting ammonia in the exhaled breath of chronic kidney disease patients.

In this experiment, all chemical reagents were analytical grade and used directly without further purification. Co3O4 NFs were synthesized using a single-nozzle electrospinning method. Firstly, 0.9 g of cobalt (II) nitrate hexahydrate and 0.9 g of polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) were separately weighed and dissolved in a solvent mixture consisting of 6 ml anhydrous ethanol and 5 ml N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF). The resulting mixture was stirred magnetically at room temperature overnight to obtain a red electrospinning precursor solution. The precursor solution was transferred to a syringe, and a voltage of 18 kV was applied at the needle tip, with a feed rate set to 0.01 ml/min and a distance of 30 cm between the needle and the collector plate. Due to the hygroscopic nature of Co3O4, it was essential to strictly control the relative humidity within the electrospinning chamber to not exceed 30 % throughout the entire electrospinning process. After 7 hours of electrospinning operation, the spun precursor samples collected on the collector plate were initially placed in a drying oven for two hours, then anneal in a muffle furnace at 300°C for 2 hours, followed by an increase in temperature to 500°C for another 2 hours at a heating rate of 2 °C/min. Co3O4 NFs were obtained through natural cooling. The preparation process of Co3O4 NFs is illustrated in Fig. 1(a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots were synthesized via a hydrothermal method as depicted in Fig. 1(b). Initially, 400 mg of L-ascorbic acid was mixed with 4 ml of ethylene glycol and 6 ml of deionized water, followed by stirring at room temperature for 10 hours. Subsequently, the mixed solution was transferred into a polytetrafluoroethylene-lined autoclave and heated at 160°C in a drying oven for 70 minutes, resulting in the formation of a CQD dispersion solution. Approximately 1.5 g of carbon quantum dots were contained in 9 ml of the CQD dispersion solution. After cooling the obtained CQD dispersion solution to room temperature, it was sealed and stored in a container for further use.

50 mg of Co3O4 nanofiber powder was placed into a mortar and mechanically ground for 10 minutes. Subsequently, 100 μl, 300 μl, and 500 μl of CQD dispersion solution were separately added dropwise, and grinding was continued for an additional 20 minutes to obtain the Co3O4/CQD composite. The resulting composites were named Co3O4/CQD100, Co3O4/CQD300, and Co3O4/CQD500, respectively. During the grinding process, when the mixture reached a homogeneous and viscous state, a brush with a diameter of 5 mm was used to evenly coat the composite onto forked finger electrodes. The specifications of the ceramic substrate were 13.4 mm×7 mm×0.635 mm, with an electrode spacing of 2 mm. The forked finger electrodes were then placed in a drying oven and dried at 60°C for 48 hours to obtain the sensor devices. The schematic diagram of the synthesis of Co3O4/CQD composite material is illustrated in Fig. 1(c).

Gas sensitivity experiments were executed through the introduction of various liquid solutions (of analytical grade) via a syringe into crucibles positioned at the periphery of the test chamber through the gas injection port. The elevated temperature within the crucibles expedited the vaporization process of the liquids. Utilizing two fans positioned at the base of the gas chamber ensured uniform dispersion of the introduced gas. Formula 1 delineates the conversion relationship between the volume of injected fluid and the resultant gas concentration[34,35].
(1)
Q = ( V × C × M ) / 22.4 × d × ρ ×
10
− 9
× ( 273 +
T
R
) / ( 273 +
T
B
)
where Q(L) represents the liquid volume, V (L) represents the volume of the testing chamber (20 L), C(ppm) represents the targeted gas concentration, M(g/mol) represents the liquid molecular weight, d represents the liquid purity, ρ(g/ml) represents the liquid density, TR (℃) is temperature of testing environment, TB (℃) is the chamber temperature.

The sensor response is quantified by the ratio of Rg to Ra resistance, where Rg represents the sensor resistance in the presence of target gases, while Ra denotes the sensor resistance in ambient air. Both the response time and recovery time are determined through a time-processing method that considers 90 % of the maximum response value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of the simulated breath samples
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the suitability of the Co3O4/CQD100 sensor for breath analysis applications, the sensor's response to simulated breath samples from chronic kidney disease patients was measured. Exhaled breath samples from six healthy volunteers were collected using Tadler bags (3 L). Subsequently, the exhaled breath samples from healthy individuals were mixed with 1.5 ppm of ammonia gas in the testing chamber of the CGS-MT to simulate the breath of chronic kidney disease patients, as shown in Fig. 2.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), specifically the Sigma 500 model, was employed to capture high-resolution images. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis, conducted with the Rigaku SmartLab SE X instrument employing Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.154176 nm), was performed to explore chemical information and crystal structure. Additionally, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis, carried out with the EscaLab 250Xi system, was utilized to ascertain the element composition and chemical state of Co3O4/CQDs.

Surface morphology and structure of pure Co3O4 nanofibers, Co3O4/CQD100, Co3O4/CQD300, and Co3O4/CQD500 were studied using SEM, and the results are presented in Fig. 3. As shown in Fig. 3(a), the surface of Co3O4 nanofibers appears rough with a porous structure, and the average diameter is approximately 80 nm. The morphology of Co3O4/CQD100, Co3O4/CQD300, and Co3O4/CQD500 is depicted in Fig. 3(b-d), where the original one-dimensional nanofibers are disrupted and fractured due to the incorporation of CQDs. The energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) images of Co3O4/CQD100 in Fig. 3(e-h) reveal the presence of C, O, and Co elements in the composite material. Moreover, the EDS spectrum in Fig. 3(i) also exhibits peaks corresponding to C, Co, and O elements. Additionally, the presence of CQD dispersion leads to hydrolysis of Co3O4 nanofibers during the composite formation process. Since the size of prepared CQD materials is extremely small, only a few nanometers, they cannot be observed directly via scanning electron microscopy. Hence, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) will be employed for the characterization of CQD materials. In the low-magnification TEM image of Co3O4/CQDs, the presence of carbon quantum dots can be observed in Fig. 3(j). Analysis of the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image in Fig. 3(k) provides information regarding the lattice fringes of carbon quantum dots, with a lattice spacing of 0.26 nanometers falling within the range of lattice spacings for carbon quantum dots (0.20–0.35 nanometers).

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis of Co3O4 and Co3O4/CQD composite material is depicted in Fig. 4. Co3O4 exhibits diffraction peaks at 2θ=18.98°, 31.28°, 36.83°, 38.75°, 44.75°, 59.36°, and 65.21°, corresponding to the (111), (220), (311), (222), (400), (511), and (440) planes, respectively. Furthermore, the broad peak observed in the XRD pattern of the composite material in the range of 26° to 28.5° corresponds to the (002) crystal plane of carbon, indicating the successful synthesis of Co3O4/CQD composite material.

To investigate the chemical composition and state of the synthesized Co3O4/CQD composite material, X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was employed for analysis. The XPS spectrum reveals three peaks, as shown in Fig. 5(a), attributed to Co 2p, C 1 s, and O 1 s. The atomic ratios of Co 2p, C 1 s, and O 1 s are 10.91 %, 40.11 %, and 48.98 %, respectively. High-resolution C1s spectrum in Fig. 5(b) confirms the presence of C-C bonds at binding energy of 284.5 eV, C-O bonds at 286.1 eV, and O-CO bonds at 288.0 eV. Furthermore, the high-resolution O1s spectrum in Fig. 5(c) displays main peaks at 529.5 eV and 532.3 eV, corresponding to Co-O and C–OH groups[36] indicating the presence of carbon-oxygen bonds in the Co3O4/CQDs composite material. In Fig. 5(d), characteristic peaks of Co 2p exhibit two spin states, Co 2p1/2 and Co 2p3/2, with peak energies at 779.70 eV and 794.40 eV associated with Co3+, and peaks at 796.10 eV and 782.30 eV attributed to Co2+ [37], confirming the coexistence of Co2+ and Co3+ in the sample. The increased content of Co2+ suggests that Co3O4/CQDs can provide more active sites, facilitating easier catalysis of oxygen[38].

To investigate the gas sensing performance of the Co3O4/CQD composite materials in this study, sensors with different doping ratios of Co3O4/CQD composites were prepared for gas sensitivity testing. Ammonia gas was chosen as the target gas, and the operating temperature of the sensors was initially studied. Fig. 6(a) presents the sensitivity of the fabricated Co3O4, Co3O4/CQD100, Co3O4/CQD300, and Co3O4/CQD500 sensors to 50 ppm ammonia gas at various operating temperatures. Among the sensors with different doping ratios of composite materials, Co3O4/CQD100 exhibits the highest response to ammonia gas, with a response value of 1.5. Compared to the Co3O4 nanofiber sensor operating at 250°C, the operating temperature of the Co3O4/CQD100 composite material is reduced by 200°C. For Co3O4/CQD300 and Co3O4/CQD500 doping ratio composite materials, the operating temperatures are 50°C and 25°C, respectively, which are significantly lower than that of the Co3O4 nanofiber. Carbon quantum dots, as important electroactive catalysts, can provide rapid intermolecular electron transfer and a large surface area, thereby reducing the power consumption of the sensor.

We tested the dynamic response of sensors with different doping ratios and Co3O4 sensors to ammonia gas concentrations ranging from 5 to 100 ppm at their respective optimal operating temperatures. The dynamic response curves are shown in Fig. 6(b). As the concentration of ammonia gas increases, the sensors exhibit higher responses. However, with the increase in carbon quantum dot doping ratio, the response values of the Co3O4/CQD100, Co3O4/CQD300, and Co3O4/CQD500 sensors gradually decrease (Fig. 6(c)). This is because a higher doping ratio of CQDs increases the static resistance of the composite material, and this effect on resistance outweighs the resistance changes in the presence of ammonia gas[33]. In Fig. 6(b), as the content of CQD doping increases, the baseline resistance (Ra) gradually increases. The Co3O4/CQDs composite material also exhibits excellent selectivity towards ammonia gas. As shown in Fig. 6(d), it is evident that Co3O4/CQDs composite materials do not respond to other gases such as ethanol, acetone, methanol, and formaldehyde, indicating a specific response only to ammonia gas. This may be attributed to the active functional groups of carbon quantum dots enhancing the adsorption of ammonia gas through specific reactions.

In Fig. 6(e), four intake and exhaust processes were tested, and the dynamic response curve of the Co3O4/CQD100 composite material to 50 ppm ammonia gas at 50°C was obtained. The dynamic response curves exhibit good consistency, indicating the device's good stability and repeatability. The response and recovery time are important criteria for evaluating whether a sensor behaves abnormally. Fig. 6(f) shows the response/recovery curve of the Co3O4/CQD100 composite material to 50 ppm ammonia gas at 50°C, with response and recovery times calculated as 14 s and 164 s, respectively. The rapid response/recovery indicates reversible surface reactions occurring throughout the gas sensing process. The one-dimensional (1D) nanostructure of Co3O4 provides a unidirectional channel for charge carrier transport and promotes the rate of electron-hole separation[39,40], thereby exhibiting excellent electronic properties.

Table 1 provides a summary of the sensing parameters of semiconductor ammonia sensors based on Co3O4 as documented in the literatures. From the data presented in Table 1, it is evident that current Co3O4-based semiconductor gas sensors struggle to simultaneously achieve low-temperature operation and rapid response and recovery. Sensors capable of exhibiting a response to ammonia at low temperatures often entail prolonged response and recovery times, with the longest reported response time being 450 s and the longest recovery time reaching 1199 s. Conversely, sensors capable of achieving rapid response and recovery invariably require higher operating temperatures, with the highest reported temperature being 200℃. Among devices capable of low-temperature operation listed in the Table 1, our sensor boasts the shortest response time, at just 14 seconds.

For practical biosensing applications, it is essential to discuss the sensing performance at low concentrations of ammonia gas. We evaluated the detection limits of Co3O4/CQD100, Co3O4/CQD300, and Co3O4/CQD500, revealing detection limits of 1.5 ppm, 5 ppm, and 5 ppm, respectively. Given that Co3O4/CQD100 exhibited the lowest detection limit, it was selected for simulating exhaled breath testing. In Fig. 7(a), the average response value of the simulated chronic kidney disease breath samples (1.0725) is close to the response value of pure biological samples (1.08) and higher than the average response value of healthy breath samples (1.0). These results confirm that the Co3O4/CQD100 sensor can distinguish between healthy individuals and chronic kidney disease patients even in the presence of significant interference from water vapor and carbon dioxide.

The resistance changes of sensors coated with Co3O4/CQD100 material within the range of 45 %-85 % relative humidity (RH) is depicted in Fig. 7(b). Despite utilizing liquids in our procedures, the volume applied per instance is at the microliter scale. Furthermore, real-time monitoring of the humidity in the evaporation chamber is conducted. No discernible fluctuations in humidity were observed upon the introduction of liquid volumes associated with all experimental procedures, thereby eliminating the impact of humidity as a confounding factor. At relative humidity of 45 %, 65 %, 75 %, and 85 %, the response values of the sensors are 1.5, 1.3, 1.24, and 1.2, respectively. The gas-sensitive element exhibits a decreasing trend in response with increasing relative humidity. This is because the increase in humidity causes water molecules to adsorb onto the surface of the gas sensor, competing with the target gas molecules for adsorption sites, thereby reducing the interaction between the target gas molecules and the sensor surface, leading to a decrease in sensor response. Additionally, as the relative humidity increases, the Ra value of the sensor shows an upward trend. This is attributed to water molecules occupying oxygen adsorption sites, resulting in a decrease in the thickness of the hole accumulation layer and an increase in resistance[48].

Regarding the long-term stability of the Co3O4/CQD100 sensor, measurements of the sensor's response and recovery to 50 ppm of ammonia gas were conducted over a period of 42 days, with a cycle of seven days. The sensor demonstrated excellent stability over this period (Fig. 7(c)). This good stability is attributed to the high chemical inertness of carbon materials[49], endowing the composite material with excellent chemical stability.

The synthesized pure Co3O4 nanofibers exhibit weak response to ammonia gas and operate at high temperatures, whereas Co3O4/CQD composite materials demonstrate a lower working temperature for ammonia gas sensing, indicating the significant impact of doped CQDs on gas sensing performance. The first step of the entire process is the adsorption of O2 on the surface, which is then reduced to O2 - ions. When carbon quantum dots and Co3O4 come into contact in the air, the holes, which are the main charge carriers, transfer from the low Fermi level of Co3O4 to the high Fermi level of carbon quantum dots until the Fermi levels reach equilibrium, forming a hole accumulation layer (HAL) as illustrated in Fig. 8 [50,51].

When the sensor is placed in an ammonia atmosphere, ammonia reacts with the O2- ions adsorbed on the material surface (Eq. 2), releasing electrons. These electrons then recombine with the holes, narrowing the hole accumulation layer and increasing the sensor's resistance. The deposition of CQDs onto Co3O4 significantly promotes the adsorption and diffusion of ammonia molecules. The interface between CQDs and Co3O4 serves as a source of defects, which are potential gas adsorption sites[52]. Therefore, compared to pure Co3O4 sensors, a large number of gas molecules can adsorb and react at the interface, resulting in a reduction in the thickness of the HAL and higher resistance in Co3O4/CQD sensors.
(2)
4
NH
3
+ 5
O
2
−
→ 4
NO + 6H
2
O + 5e
−

CQDs deposited on Co3O4 can form a heterojunction potential barrier at the interface between CQDs and Co3O4, greatly facilitating the adsorption and diffusion of ammonia molecules. Carbon quantum dots possess active functional groups such as hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl groups on their surfaces[53]. Some of these functional groups are retained through the incomplete carbonization of small organic molecules, while others are modified into carbon quantum dots through surface activation reactions. Since doping and testing processes are conducted at relatively low temperatures (50°C), many incompletely substituted hydroxyl groups on the surface of CQDs are preserved to the maximum extent possible. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) analysis confirms the presence of hydroxyl groups in Co3O4/CQDs, which can react with ammonia as follows[54]:
(3)
R − OH +
NH
3
→ R −
NH
2
+
H
2
O

Therefore, ammonia molecules transfer more electrons to Co3O4/CQDs compared to other molecules. The greater the electron transfer, the larger the resistance change in Co3O4/CQDs, resulting in higher response values. Additionally, oxygen in ethanol and acetone exhibits lower nucleophilicity than nitrogen in ammonia, causing less hindrance to ammonia compared to ethanol and acetone[55]. This strongest interaction between ammonia and Co3O4/CQDs contributes to the highest selectivity for ammonia among the tested gases.

Furthermore, the abundance of active functional groups on CQDs provides additional reaction sites for ammonia molecules, increasing their adsorption capacity and thereby enhancing the material's gas sensing performance.

The morphology of materials is a crucial factor influencing gas sensing performance. Materials with a porous microstructure possess a larger specific surface area, i.e., greater surface energy[56]. A larger surface energy often leads to increased adsorption of oxygen molecules and target gas molecules. Consequently, more surface gas-sensitive reactions occur, resulting in a greater gas sensing response[33]. In the case of Co3O4/CQDs composite materials, the introduction of CQDs increases the material's specific surface area, enhancing the contact area between ammonia gas and the material surface, thus increasing gas adsorption.

In this study, Co3O4 nanofibers and CQDs were synthesized separately using electrospinning and hydrothermal methods, respectively, and then CQDs were doped into Co3O4 via grinding to synthesize Co3O4/CQDs composite materials. Compared to Co3O4 nanofibers, the Co3O4/CQDs composite material exhibits superior gas sensing performance towards ammonia, not only operating at lower temperatures but also demonstrating outstanding selectivity towards ammonia. The optimal response to ammonia was observed when 50 mg of Co3O4 was doped with 100 µl of CQDs. The response time of the Co3O4/CQD100 sensor to 50 ppm of ammonia at the operating temperature was 14 s. Additionally, the feasibility of using the Co3O4/CQD100 sensor to detect the exhaled breath of chronic kidney disease patients was simulated, and the results confirmed that the sensor can distinguish between the exhaled breath of diseased and healthy individuals. Introducing carbon quantum dot materials into Co3O4 may provide new insights for enhancing ammonia sensing.

Jiapeng Xu: Writing – original draft. Chaoqiang Liu: Investigation, Data curation. Lijuan Fu: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Wei Tang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Qingyu Liu: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (61801276), and Shandong Province Natural Science Foundation (ZR2018BF003)
